prompt: Write a short passage about a stranger arriving in a quiet town.
text: The protagonist is an old man with white hair and a mustache, who has just been released from prison after serving six years for manslaughter. His name is William Smith, and he comes to the small town looking for somewhere quiet to spend his remaining days. He rents a room at a small inn that is run by an elderly couple named Tom and Sarah Brown. As soon as they see him walk into the bar, their eyes light up with excitement. "Ah, there's our little William!" they say. "How nice! How nice it is to see you." They lead him to the back kitchen where they serve him wine. Over the next few weeks, the Browns become a constant presence in William's life. They take him out for walks every morning, and always carry some of the best produce from the local market. One day, while he was sitting on the steps listening to the wind blowing through the tree-tops, he heard a noise coming from the garden. It was his friend Henry Jones, whom he hadn't seen since he had left school nearly ten years ago. For a moment he felt ashamed to admit that he knew him, but then he remembered what a good friend he had once been and decided not to talk about it. After dinner one evening,

prompt: Write a short passage about a stranger arriving in a quiet town.
text: The person looks at you, and you feel the need to tell them that they are not welcome here. You try hard to remain unharsh, but find yourself unable to do so. You want to be as kind as you can possibly be. The stranger has gone into his house, and is sitting there reading, apparently in deep thought. He must have been here only a little while, for it was quite dark when he came out, and I had just got back from dinner. There were stars shining in the sky, and the moonlight fell upon the old church-stone wall which runs along one side of the street. It looked like some broken part of an ancient tower that had fallen off and floated down with the tide. It was very strange. A sense of loneliness gripped me, and made me think that perhaps this man would never come out again. I went indoors to light a candle, and sit down by the fire. At the end of ten minutes or so, when I had taken my hat and coat on, and turned out the lamp, the door opened, and the man re-entered. He sat down beside me, took up the book he had left, and began to read over it once more with great earnestness. His face seemed
